msDesc.head: John Capgrave, commentary on Exodus; King’s Lynn, ?1440

msItem.author: John Capgrave
msItem.title: Commentarium in Exodum
msItem.incipit: [fol. 3va] Hec sunt nomina filiorum … Quia historiam quam hic prosequi intendit
msItem.explicit: [fol. 186rb] tabernaculi sui obumbracionem idem dominus noster christus ihesus. Amen. |
msItem.finalRubric: [display script:] Incepit hoc opus Frater Iohannes Capgrave In festo sancti Antonij confessor que occurrit mense Januarii Anno domini moccccmoxxxviij et fecit finem in festo sancti Johannis ante portam latinam Anno domini moccccmoxlo Feliciter.
msItem.note: The preface alone edited by Lucas and Dalton, ‘Capgrave’s Preface’. Lower half of final column originally blank.
Fol. 186v: plan of the temple preceded by text, 17 lines each in 2 columns, now rubbed to illegibility.

supportDesc.support: Fairly thick parchment (skin, hair-hair, skin-skin). Fairly often, the leaves are not fully rectangular (e.g. fol. 11, 12, 21 etc). Some tears and blemishes to the parchment, occasionally with original repairs (e.g. fol. 6, 26, 37, 46, 91, 162); the top half of one folio has been removed (fol. 157).
supportDesc.extent:
iii + 184 + ii leaves. All flyleaves, except third at front, added with binding, with first and last marbled on the outer side in style of binding; the third front flyleaf is loose card, added (s. xx2) to protect opening leaf of text.
405 269
supportDesc.foliation: Foliation (s. xx) added at top right in pencil: 1–187, including first two front flyleaves and first of the final flyleaves.
supportDesc.collation: Quires of eight: i–xxiii(8). Horizontal catchwords placed c. 40mm beneath text in inner margin, on each occasion within a large scroll design. Leaf signatures throughout in one continuous series (e.g. fol. 6 = ‘+iij’; fol. 11 = aj; fol. 28 = cij; fol. 37 = diij; fol. 46 = eiiij; fol. 156 = tiij; fol. 166 = viiij; fol. 171 = xj).

layoutDesc.layout: Fol. 19 = (38 + [272] + 95) (27 + [76 + 18 + 74] + 74) Fully ruled in brown, with all verticals, as well as top two and bottom two horizontals reaching the page’s edge, as do two extra horizontals in upper margin for running headers (6mm apart, lower 15mm above top line), and two extra verticals in outer margin (10mm apart, inner 10mm from bounding line), used occasionally to distinguish between the three types of commentary (‘hist[orice]’, ‘mor[aliter]’, ‘All[egorice]’ but, at fol. 80–140, ‘mist[ice]’ and, at fol. 148, both these terms used). Full pricking repeatedly visible at very edge of outer margin, with prick-holes also visible at top and bottom in line with the verticals. 54 lines to the column.

handDesc.summary: A single hand deploying two scripts. This scribe also wrote the companion volume, Oxford: Oriel College, MS. 32. While there are differences between Lucas, ‘Scribe and Publisher’ and Colledge, ‘Capgrave’s “Autographs”’, over which manuscripts Capgrave himself produced, both agree that these are not by him. They also agree in claiming that the interlinear corrections are by Capgrave, but while these are necessarily smaller and so less set, there is very little in ductus and letter-forms to distinguish them from the scribe who may, therefore, be acting as corrector.
handDesc.handNote: For the Biblical passages, a textura quadrata, albeit the placing of the feet is not fully regular (minim height: 2mm, while ascenders occupy nearly the full 4mm between lines)

For the commentary, a small textualis (minim height: 1mm; ascenders: 3mm), with rounded feet and double-bowled a being less square, but retaining the prominent approach stroke on ascenders. Change of ink but not script at fol. 75ra, l. 18. Biblical passages are underlined in red and open with a blue one-line initial (guide letter often visible beneath); majuscules in the passages and commentary red-slashed.

decoDesc.decoNote: The book opens with one three-sided border at fol. 3r, and another at fol. 3v. The first has a 14-line inhabited initial, half the width of the inner column, depicting the kneeling author presenting his volume (depicted as bound in red with a central clip and clasp) to a seated Duke Humfrey; the second has an 8-line initial, third of the column width, with the blue ‘h’ inhabited by an acanthus in green and pink, all on a gold background. Scott emphasised the ‘density of motif, with spraywork that is virtually impenetrable’ of the first border and commented that the second border ‘seems to come from a different world from the first’ [Dated and Datable Borders, pp. 61–62]. Her conclusion was that they may be by the same artist; it might, however, be posited that the difference in style suggests they were produced at different sittings, with the first possibly added after the author was confident the presentation to his patron was guaranteed.

A few illuminations interrupt the text, the opening ones each in gold, outlined in black: at fol. 114ra-rb, two depictions of the Ark of the Covenant supported by two angels with blue haloes (four-winged in the first, two-winged in the second); at fol. 118vb, two depictions of the showbread table (the shulhan); at fol. 122ra, the menorah; fol. 125ra, the Temple curtain, in pink, white and green, outlined in gold; fol. 126rb, the Tabernacle curtain, in brown to depict coarse wool (not wooden boards as Index of Images has it); fol. 127v, outer margin, two depictions of boards of the Tabernacle, in brown and grey; fol. 130, full width, a side of the Tabernacle, in brown, grey and gold; fol. 133vb, Altar of the Holocaust, in black with red flames, silver grill and gold chains. At fol. 186v, occupying lower half of the folio, a ground-plan of the Temple, outlined in gold. Scott relates these images to those for Nicholas of Lyre’s Postillae in Oxford: Bodleian, MS. Laud. misc. 156 [Tradition and Innovation, p. 28 with fig. 24 reproducing fol. 114rb].

Chapters open with filigree initials, blue letter on red flourishing, usually five or six lines high but sometimes fewer based on space available (fol. 7vb, 11rb, 16va, 21va, 23vb, 25vb [3-line], 28vb, 31va, 34vb, 37ra, 38va, 46rb, 51rb, 55va [4-line], 63va [3-line], 70va, 73va, 76rb, 79ra [4-line], 92ra, 98va, 103vb, 107vb [4-line], 111ra, 131vb, 136rb [4-line], 144ra, 148rb, 154ra, 155rb, 160rb, 164rb [4-line], 169va, 173rb, 175vb, 178ra [4-line with a bearded and hatted face, a trefoil emerging from his mouth], 180vb, 183va [a bearded and floppy-hatted face, a banderole emerging from his mouth]).

binding.p: Leather over pasteboards, with gilt fillet (s. xix), both boards now adrift. Sewn on six bands. On spine in second compartment from top, a black leather label embossed in gold: ‘Explicatio Exodi M.S’. At inside of upper board, the armorial book-plate of ‘Sir Robert Throckmorton Baronet’ (see HISTORY) [copy of this bookplate in E. Almack, Bookplates (London, 1904), p. 50].

history.provenance: Humfrey, duke of Gloucester: this manuscript does not have an ex libris by Humfrey, in contrast to both its sister manuscript, Oriel MS. 32, and most volumes which were presented to the duke. It may be that his handwriting appeared on a now-lost flyleaf; it is not to be found elsewhere in the book. The volume was certainly dedicated to him and passed from him to the University of Oxford, being recorded in the 1444 donation [Thomson, UO3.20]. The implication is that he must have owned this for less than half a decade, given that the colophon records the date of completion as 6th May 1440 (and this could be of composition rather than production). That it should come to live in Oxford was appropriate, given Capgrave’s reference in his preface to the duke’s generosity in giving books to the University.

Oxford, Oxfordshire, University (see above). It may be that some of the marginalia in the manuscript reflect its use while in the University’s possession; one small mid-fifteenth century script appears at fol. 42 and 63 (?cf. fol. 17).

In the second quarter of the sixteenth century, it and the volume now Oriel MS. 32 remained together in Oxford but had moved into private hands: one reader annotates both volumes (in this, fol. 3v–7v, 9, 10, 13, 13v, 16v, 17, 18, 19, 19v, 21, 22v, 25v–39v, 40v–45v, 46v–48, 48v, 52v–76v, 77v–79, 80–108, 111v–117, 120, 120v). In this codex, this owner also added a set of verses below the colophon at fol. 186 ‘in laudem insignissimi doctoris Cartewritus [sic]’ and in praise of ‘Grimaldus’. The first has been identified as Nicholas Cartwright (d. 1559), who had been a fellow of Magdalen College until 1530 but this must have been written after 10 July 1536 when he received his D.Th.; the latter must be the playwright Nicholas Grimald (d. c. 1563) — reference is made to the theatre in the poem — who was incorporated at Oxford in 1542, having been educated in Cambridge.

The next stage in the life of the manuscript is unclear. If it stayed with its sister manuscript, it may have remained in Oxford, as that was given to Oriel in the seventeenth century. This volume, however, has a feature missing from its companion: there is damage from rubbing and damp at the outermost leaves of the original manuscript (fol. 1 and fol. 186v), suggesting that the manuscript was left unbound for a time.

The next certain owner of the manuscript was the Throckmorton family, who kept it in the Library of Buckland House, then Berkshire, now Oxfordshire. The book-plate at the front of the binding has been taken to be that of the 4th baronet (1702–91; cf. MS. Don. b. 5). The book was certainly in the family’s library before his death, as it is recorded in the late eighteenth-century catalogues of the Buckland Library: MS. Eng. misc. b. 13, fol. 6 and 53v (‘Explicatio Exodi M.S. a Joanne Capgravio’; shelfmark: D2; catalogue begun post-1787 [fol. 4] but finished post-1790 [fol. 6v]), 91 (‘Explicatio Exodi M.S. Illumin’d, by Capgrave’; B, shelf 2; catalogue post-1782 [fol. 100]). It is then the ‘Exodi Explicatio MSS’ (shelfmark: D3) in the 1796 catalogue which opens MS. Eng. misc. b. 12, fol. 18v, 65.
It was sold by the Throckmorton family at auction in Sotheby’s rooms on 11th November 1896 for £21 5s [Schoenberg Database, no. 284498].
history.acquisition: It was subsequently purchased by the Bodleian in 1897 from ‘messrs. Karslake & Co. of London (Catal. 2 = 88, no. 141)’ [Summary Catalogue; see also Pietas Oxoniensis in Memory of Sir Thomas Bodley, Knt. and the Foundation of the Bodleian Library (Oxford, 1902), p. 11n.

recordHist.source:
Description by David Rundle, 2026. Previously described: Summary Catalogue, Vol. 6, p. 148
Summary Catalogue, Vol. 6, p. 149

ref.title: MLGB3: Medieval Libraries of Great Britain

listBibl.head: Printed descriptions:
listBibl.bibl: A. Watson, Catalogue of Dated and Datable Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries (1984), no. 484

listBibl.head: Other bibliography:
listBibl.bibl: L. Dennison et al., An Index of Images in English Manuscripts. The Bodleian Library, Oxford, fascicle II (Turnhout, 2001), p. 45.
P. J. Lucas, ‘John Capgrave O.S.A. (1393–1464) Scribe and Publisher’, TCBS, v (1969), pp. 1–35.
P. J. Lucas and R. Dalton, ‘Capgrave’s Preface dedicating his commentary In Exodum to Humfrey duke of Gloucester’, BodLR, xi (1982), pp. 20–25.
K. L. Scott, Dated and Datable English Manuscript Borders, c. 1395–1499 (London, 2002), pp. 60–63.
ead., Tradition and Innovation in Later Medieval Manuscripts (London, 2007), p. 28.

revisionDesc.change: Add extent, leaf dimensions and layout from Watson, Dated and Datable.
James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
